     async with finalize(some_generator) as c:
             for n in c:

Hi @jdmarch, I've added support for this endpoint. Here is a quick example of how it can be used:

```python
audit_generator = zenpy_client.tickets.audits()
for audit in audit_generator:
    print(audit)

# You can retrieve the cursor values from the spent generator.
print(audit_generator.after_cursor, audit_generator.before_cursor)

# You can pass an explicit cursor value to consume audits create after that point.
for audit in zenpy_client.tickets.audits(cursor='fDE1MTc2MjkwNTQuMHx8'):
    print(audit)

# Reversing the generator reverses the direction in which you consume objects. The
# following grabs objects from just before the cursor value until the beginning of time.
for audit in reversed(zenpy_client.tickets.audits(cursor='fDE1MTc2MjkwNTQuMHx8')):
    print(audit)
```

Hi Yoann,

I implemented a fix for this in the latest develop version. Would you mind trying that and see if it fixes your issue?

The fix removes all contigs with 0 in coverage before calculating average coverage. Contigs with 0 coverage (no mappings) will, by definition, not be scaffolded anyway. I consequently needed to change the output to be coverage estimates of "all contigs with at least one good mapping".  

Hopefully this will also fix Dan's issue with the first histogram shown above. Too many contigs with 0 coverage will make the "Removing outlier" algorithm converge to 0.

> how to deal with fit parameters

Hm, that's a good question ... 

- `n_classes`: I guess we don't have to worry about `n_classes` as that shouldn't change. 
- `n_features`: We don't have to worry about that in the 1st level classifiers as the k-folds should have the same feature dimension as the training set. However, it's a bit tricky regarding the 2nd-level (meta) classifier. Maybe we should add a check in the `fit` method for that, for example,

```python
if not self.use_features_in_secondary:
    n_features_in_2nd = len(self.classifiers)

else:
    n_features_in_2nd += X.shape[1]

if 'coef_init' in meta_fit_params and\
        meta_fit_params['coef_init'].shape[1] != n_features_in_2nd:
    raise AttributeError('Number of features in the `fit_params`'s `coef_init` array'
                         ' of the meta-classifier must be equal to'
                         ' the number of features this classifier expects based on the'
                         ' number of first-level classifiers and wether `use_features_in_secondary`
                         ' is set to `True` or `False`.'
                         ' Expected: %d'
                         ' Got: %d' % (n_features_in_2nd, meta_fit_params['coef_init'].shape[1]))
```

On the other hand, there are probably multiple parameters that use shape`n_features` somehow. And my guess is it would be quite tricky to maintain this. 

- `n_samples`: this is probably most tricky since we are modifying the training set in a sense, and like you said, we kind of need a way to determine when to use the subindices to pass the correct weights.

I agree with you that an automated checking based on the shape is quite unstable and might break in certain edge cases. Hm, I currently don't have a good idea for how to handle that.

For now, maybe we should just handle `sample_weight` explicitly via passing the respective the training set indices and be very clear about that in the `fit` docstring that and mention that any other parameter based on `sample_weights` shapes (and `n_features` for the meta-classifier) might result in unexpected behavior?

I just double-checked the DBus specification and pre-authentication
synchronization is not required. It sounds like the dbus daemon included in
Centos 5 is buggy in that regard. It's good to know you found a workaround
and were able to get up and running but I'm not sure it makes sense to
modify the txdbus implementation to support a buggy daemon on an
out-of-date platform. It could be done, of course, possibly as an optional
"centos5_auth_bug_workaround=True" flag on the connect method but I'd need
some convincing that this is a good idea.

Tom

Through multiple tests, I found out that this error occurs on arm32 (armhf) bit architecture.
And on arm64 (aarch64) does not arise.
This is due to the fact that for armhf and for aarch64 the type long has different length values.


For aarch64:
sizeof (long) = 8
sizeof (long long) = 8

For armhf
sizeof (long) = 4
sizeof (long long) = 8

The only thing there is is the gradient.  Those loss values are just for display basically and don't feed into SGD.  So you can't make the gradient not match the objective function.  It's just impossible given how the code is structured.  

Maybe the loss can be changed.  But it's not obvious and requires a lot of experimentation to find something better.  

If you want to get more detections you should just change the detection threshold.  Try values and see what happens.  Or plot a ROC curve and pick a threshold by looking at that.

I got the same error:
Loss has been increasing, reloading saved state from training_metric.sync
pure virtual method called
terminate called without an active exception

Well, everything in SNMP is ultimately encoded into BER... Including SNMP tables, they are encoded as a sequence of variable-bindings ordered on the column-basis.

If you want to serialize anything with BER, you need to express it as ASN.1 data structure. SNMP tables are "imaginary", they are just ordered variable-bindings. In the standard they call these tables as "conceptual" probably referring to the fact that there is no solid 2-D data structure behind them.

I think you need to explain (or figure out) how exactly do you want SNMP tables to be BER-serialized.

select.select() can also be used to check if sockets are in an error state.  (If your design is to check sockets one at a time, there isn't any particular advantage however.)

It is generally good practice to try to shutdown() sockets before closing (this allows the server to free resources faster.)
socket.close() may raise exceptions.  Especially if it is an SSL socket, they will often do an SSL protocol shutdown handshake on close.

Which also brings up that close may timeout or take a long time :-)  (It will call SSL_shutdown https://www.openssl.org/docs/ssl/SSL_shutdown.html) 

This stuff gets VERY tricky.  I've not attempted to write code to solve it in the general case, only for a known model of concurrency (gevent), which I am also able to control all of the "socket-like" things that go in.

I'd advise against compiling code using `fake_libc_include`.  This file is intended strictly to help pycparser parse the code a little easier.  For example, all the sized-integer types like `int8_t` are just typedef'd to `int`, which is definitely _not_ what you want.

It looks like you want to programmatically modify the source code before compiling?  This would be tricky with `pycparser`, perhaps using the location information in the AST to modify a copy of the original source file on disk.  I haven't personally done this.

I concede that my particular use case is far from robust w.r.t. Python packaging and that I'm partially at fault for falling into this trap. However, basic run-time detection for compatibility is a good idea and I think having something basic in psutil's implementation is warranted. Other scenarios where you can fall into this trap include when you've obtained the psutil source code and you encounter an error compiling the C extensions but you still have the .py files installed and the C extensions from your system install are utilized. Packaging is complicated. This stuff does happen.

There is precedence in other Python C extensions for this. Here's PIL's implementation: https://github.com/python-pillow/Pillow/commit/088c752e4049767b3575fb8bb3c24b1190c5ad62 As you can see, it isn't that much work.

Also, you don't need to hard-code the version tuple in each C extension module: you can `#define` it in a shared file and `#include` that from each C extension module. With a little setup.py magic, it might even be possible to auto-generate that shared file from whatever version is present in setup.py or a `__version__` variable in another file.

I often add to the DB API specification, but I'm a bit concerned about deviating from it too much.  We should consider that.

Also, I really hate to add *anything* else to the parameters until they are fixed up.  I completely abrogated responsibility a few months back and merged in the fast executemany with a cursory test but without going over the code.  There are now *two* sections of code for binding parameters for each type and they do not use the same rules at all.  For example, the string binding assumes every driver is using UCS2 which is absolutely not the case.  Even though it is what the specification says, I spent a lot of effort writing version 4.x to add encoding support so it works with all databases.

Similarly, Decimal support was set to a string because so few databases get the binary part right the last time I tried.  (Including well known ones like Oracle, IIRC.)

I started a branch to consolidate, but I was derailed by a bunch of other projects and would need to start fresh.  I'm not sure the best place to have a conversation about that.

Understood. I guess the problem you'll have here is that the translation into the `lib.yangtypes` object is not always going to maintain all information. For example, if you have a `uint64` that subsequently has another set of restrictions - then there's nothing that ensures that all the information from the more restricted class is available to someone examining the class. It's possible to add it, but it also means making the types that are generated more complex to ensure that they are always transparent down to the last value. I'd question whether this optimisation is actually worth it.

In terms of storing as a JSON object in a database - Postgres has a cool set of features since it has a way that one can structure some queries against a field that is defined as `jsonb`. For example, see http://www.postgresql.org/docs/9.4/static/functions-json.html or http://schinckel.net/2014/05/25/querying-json-in-postgres/.

I'd definitely be interested in seeing the SQL schema that you've generated/the tooling once you're done!

Well, in my use case, pausing observer is needed as i want to calculate progress of files(byte size) every 10 seconds for eg. If i will implement it with sleep(10) inside EventHandler it will calculate progress even there are no changes on directory, leading to endless calculation.
For me this code portion worked:
```python
class PausingObserver(Observer):
    def dispatch_events(self, *args, **kwargs):
        if not getattr(self, '_is_paused', False):
            super(PausingObserver, self).dispatch_events(*args, **kwargs)

    def pause(self):
        self._is_paused = True

    def resume(self):
        time.sleep(10)  # allow interim events to be queued
        self.event_queue.queue.clear()
        self._is_paused = False
```


And from now we can use pause and resume for eg, with on_created() event:

```python

class ProgressEstimate(FileSystemEventHandler):
def on_created(self, event):
        observer.pause()
        observer.resume()
        dest_folder_size = self.get_size_of_folder(event.src_path)
        print("Backup folder size {}".format(dest_folder_size))
```

The main section:

```python
if __name__ == "__main__":
    
    logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO,
                        format='%(asctime)s - %(message)s',
                        datefmt='%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S')

    dest_path = sys.argv[1]
    observer = PausingObserver()
    event_handler = ProgressEstimate()
    observer.schedule(event_handler, dest_path, recursive=True)
    observer.start()
    try:
        while True:
            time.sleep(1)
    except KeyboardInterrupt:
        observer.stop()
    observer.join()
```

Sample code below:

[https://github.com/cablehead/python-consul/blob/master/consul/base.py#L58](https://github.com/cablehead/python-consul/blob/master/consul/base.py#L58)

```python
from six.move import urllib_parse

url = urllib_parse.quote(url, safe='/:')
ret = {'http': url, 'interval': interval}
```

I think these are the only options:

- Parse the progress lines from stderr: https://github.com/bramp/ffmpeg-cli-wrapper/issues/21
- Listening on some port for progress notices from `ffmpeg`: https://stackoverflow.com/a/31353647

From `man ffmpeg`:
```
-progress url (global)
   Send program-friendly progress information to url.

   Progress information is written approximately every second and at the end of the
   encoding process. It is made of "key=value" lines. key consists of only alphanumeric
   characters. The last key of a sequence of progress information is always "progress".
```

To implement either of these, the programs that use this library need to be structured for some degree of concurrency.

The progress checking code either needs to be run asynchronously or in another thread. In the first case for obvious reasons: you constantly need to poll the socket for incoming connections; in the second case to avoid filling up the stderr buffer in case you wait for too long between one poll and another, unless you're okay with ffmpeg randomly getting stuck.

This can be done with `threading.Thread`, `twisted`, `asyncio` or anything else, probably except `multiprocessing` (having a separate process do the polling thing would totally be an overkill and would only make things more complicated).

```python
text = re.sub(r'[^\x00-\x7F]+',' ', text)
```

to replace any non-ascii characters with a space, but then this may not be very acceptable if there are accented characters you want to show.

Alternatively, perhaps try:

```
text.encode('latin-1')
```

to try and convert the UTF8 / unicode into the latin character set.

First just a side note about your sample code:

```python
    for i, existing_encoding in enumerate(faces):
        if face_recognition.compare_faces([existing_encoding], encoding)[0]:
            return i
```

That could potentially be much faster written like this:

```python
matches = face_recognition.compare_faces(faces, encoding)
first_match_index = matches.index(True)
```
... because that would take advantage of any parallel processing capabilities of your CPU using numpy behind the scenes. Doing it in a `for` loop will be a lot slower in practice on most CPUs unless you have tons of duplicate faces in your dataset (where the early return might help).

Now back to your real question:

The incremental part is tricky. I can think of some esoteric ideas to try, but I don't have any good working examples of clustering unknown faces in an incremental way.

dlib itself provides [an implementation of the Chinese Whispers algorithm which can be used to cluster unknown faces](http://dlib.net/face_clustering.py.html) fairly quickly using a graph data structure.  In the graph, each face is a node and you connect each node to any other node where it's euclidean distance is less than 0.6 (or whatever threshold you choose).

Unfortunately dlib doesn't expose the underlying graph data structure from the python api so you can't add more nodes (faces) to it on-the-fly. You can do it from [dlib's C++ api though](http://dlib.net/dnn_face_recognition_ex.cpp.html). Alternately you could use a different implementation of Chinese Whispers in python that lets you access the graph and add nodes to the graph structure on-the-fly and implement your own version of it.

However at the end of the day I don't think that will actually be faster than your current approach because you still have to check the new face you add to the graph against every existing node to add it to the graph. But it would probably do a better job at determining the number of actually unique people in the dataset instead of just assuming the first match you find is the best match.

Hello,

You should be able to just put it in a filter like you did, but only if the queryset it is passed is translation-aware queryset. This is what you should get by specifying it on your view:

```python
class CollectionsRetrieveUpdate(generics.RetrieveUpdateAPIView):
    queryset = Collection.objects.language()
```

This will make DRF's default `get_queryset()` return a translation-aware queryset.
Actually, if your backend uses django's `LocaleMiddleware`, then the language will be automatically set to the request's detected language (see here [Django's language detection](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.11/topics/i18n/translation/#how-django-discovers-language-preference). Most notably, assuming you use separate domains for your main site and your API, you can use HTTP header `Accept-Language` from your frontend.

@jsma I managed to get it working.

In a Django shell, if you do:

```python
from django.apps import apps

for app in apps.get_app_configs():
    print(app, app.name, app.label)
```

You will see the proper `app.name` that is required by `is_installed`

For example, if you have `project.some_app.apps.SomeAppConfig` in your `INSTALLED_APPS` then `project.some_app` is going to be the proper name that will work for `COMMENTS_APP`

I tried using the example described in the docstring:
```python
>>> cond = lambda i, s: i <= 5
>>> func = lambda i, s: ([i + s], [i + 1, s + i])
>>> loop_vars = (mx.sym.var('i'), mx.sym.var('s'))
>>> outputs, states = mx.sym.contrib.while_loop(cond, func, loop_vars, max_iterations=10)
```
I am evaluating this symbolic graph like this to inspect the outputs from each iteration:
```python
mx.sym.Group(outputs + states).eval(i=mx.nd.zeros((1,)), s=mx.nd.ones((1,)))[0].asnumpy().astype('int32')
```

I've added documentation for the callbacks methods and added a new callback method called ``bless_models``.
The new callback is in charge of tagging models discovered during spec traversing.
The difference between ``tag_models`` and ``bless_models`` is that:
 * ``tag_models`` tags models present on ``...#/definitions`` defaulting the model name (if not present) to the definition ``key``
 * ``bless_models`` tags all the models identified during spec traversing (inline models). If an inline model is present but no model name is assigned (``x-model`` and ``title`` are missing) no tag will be added

ROUTER-ROUTER is vulnerable to the fact that it takes a finite amount of time for the handshake to propagate identities. The `ROUTER_PROBE` socket option is designed to help with such things. It triggers a send of an empty message on connect, which the server can reply to, completing a handshake.

Here is a modified version that never loses messages:

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3

import sys
import zmq


def main(me, target=None):
    """
    Ubuntu 14.04 LTS, with misc. package updates.

    $ apt show libzmq | grep Version
    Version: 4.2.0-1
    $ uname -spr
    Linux 3.16.7-ckt11-061515+ x86_64

    Run in two terms like:
    term1$ python3 ./zmqtest.py T1
    term2$ python3 ./zmqtest.py T2 T1
    """

    print('Python version:', sys.version)          # 3.5.2
    print('libzmq version:', zmq.zmq_version())    # 4.2.0
    print('PyZMQ version :', zmq.pyzmq_version())  # 16.0.2

    ctx = zmq.Context()
    socket = ctx.socket(zmq.ROUTER)
    socket.identity = me.encode('utf-8')

    if not target:
        socket.bind('tcp://*:2060')

        print('waiting for messages...')
        while True:
            msg = socket.recv_multipart()
            if msg[1] == b'':
                # send ACK for connection probes
                print('connection from', msg[0])
                socket.send_multipart(msg)
            else:
                print(msg)

    else:
        def connect():
            # probe triggers an empty send on connect
            socket.probe_router = 1
            socket.connect('tcp://localhost:2060')
            # wait for probe reply before sending
            msg = socket.recv_multipart()
            print('connected to', msg[0])

        target = target.encode('utf-8')
        connect()

        # All of these messages will arrive:
        socket.send_multipart([target, b'HELLO #1'])
        for i in range(100):
            socket.send_multipart([target, b'HELLO #2'])

        socket.send_multipart([target, b'HELLO #3'], copy=False)

    socket.close(linger=0)
    ctx.term()

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main(*sys.argv[1:])
```

i'm somewhat biased towards the later approach since imo the whole python glue code is a bit ugly

runtime of the new test went from 15s to 6s after the fix

the speedup should be a lot more though since there's some serial overhead (Amdahl's law)

The requests seem reasonable to me. Marking as a feature request. Just addressing each one of them.

>> There's no way to get at the list of ObjectVersions for an Object. One has to do an inefficient Bucket.object_versions.all() to list all object versions. I suggest adding a Object.versions.all() to list all versions of an object. This call can make a more efficient call to list_object_versions giving the prefix as the object key to limit the results to versions of this particular object.

This seems reasonable, but we would need to add a custom method (and not via our normal resource models) because the `S3.list_object_versions` does not let you specify just a key. There is only an option for prefixes so we would need to filter out objects that do not match the key name exactly.

>> Given an ObjectVersion, there's no way to get at the ACL for this version. One can only get the ACL for an Object. ACLs are version specific. I suggest adding a ObjectVersion.Acl() sub-resource.

Makes sense as well. I believe it would only require a resource model update to add.

>> Object from an ObjectVersion somehow gets me to the latest version of an Object and not to the specific version. So, I can't simply create an Object from an ObjectVersion and try to get its Acl.

I am not sure if we will be able to get that workflow to work. Specifically because the identifiers for an Object are the bucket and key. So internally we will not proxy the version id to the object. Ideally, the `ObjectVersion.Acl()` would be the workflow we would add and suggest people to use.

So just to fully close the loop here, you can now specify anything you like to `--python` and it will be prepended to the resulting `pyz` as your shebang. 

The value provided to `--python` should be anything _after_ `#!`, so for example:

    shiv -c pipenv -o ~/bin/pipenv --python "/usr/bin/env python3.6" pipenv pew

or
    shiv -c pipenv -o ~/bin/pipenv --python "/opt/alternate/python3 -sE" pipenv pew

Both will work and do not require the targeted shebang to exist on the local filesystem.

Thanks for the additional information!  To answer your question, older versions of pyvcloud defined an Http and a Log class in pyvcloud/__init__.py.  That's why you were able to import them. We removed these as part of exposing a clean API layer focused on vCD operations.  They are no longer part of pyvcloud.   You can see the history of the changes in git using the following command: 

   git log -p pyvcloud/__init__.py

If your applications needs the functions from these classes for its own use, you should be able to get them by importing logger and request normally.  If you are extending pyvcloud itself, have a look in client.py for current usage.

There are only two real options on the table for the abstract dependency declarations: the Python-based DSL and TOML. Other config languages are out at the ecosystem level due either to simple inconsistency with the decision in PEP 518, or else for the specific reasons that PEP 518 chose TOML over the other alternatives that were considered.

As pretty as I think the Python-based DSL is, I'm struggling to see it ending well given the other factors at play (most notably, the fact that we're dealing with a 25+ year old language ecosystem, and an 18+ year old packaging ecosystem):
- if we don't restrict it, then we can expect lots of folks to treat it the way they treat `setup.py` today, with all of the problems that entails (i.e. the 18+ year old packaging ecosystem problem)
- if we do restrict it, then we break the intuitions of a lot of folks that are going to expect a Python-like configuration syntax used in a Python project to actually *be* Python (i.e. the 25+ year old language ecosystem problem)

User inertia is an incredibly powerful force, and we've seen plenty of ecosystem improvement efforts run into problems by failing to grant it adequate respect (most notably `distutils2`).

The other problem I haven't seen being considered so far with the Python-based DSL is "Which version of Python?". The examples given so far have all been Python 2/3 compatible, but what if someone wanted to write:

    INTERNAL_HOST = "https://our.repository.manager.example.com/python/"
    source(f"{INTERNAL_HOST}/production")
    source(f"{INTERNAL_HOST}/staging")
    source(f"{INTERNAL_HOST}/dev")

People doing things like that would mean that not only does a consumer of the file need to be using Python 3.6 to generate `Pipfile.lock`, they would *also* need to be using it for any analysis of the abstract dependencies at all.

By contrast, if the baseline format for abstract dependency declarations is TOML 0.4.0, then folks that really want an imperative format can still do that (e.g. by defining a `Pipfile.in` or `Pipfile.py` file), but the clear expectation would be that they still need to generate a declarative `Pipfile` and check it in. Since the decision to use such tools (or not) would be made on a project-by-project basis, then it would be up to the tool authors to decide whether or not they maintained sole control over the generated `Pipfile`, or whether they supported interoperability with both other automated tools and manual editing.

(Note that to facilitate the latter, we could potentially borrow a concept from `pyproject.toml` and require tools to put their automatically managed entity declarations under a `tool.<name>` namespace. It would require folks actually processing the raw `Pipfile` to merge the different tool contributions together, but anyone solely consuming `Pipfile.lock` still wouldn't need to worry about where the different dependencies originated)

Thanks for reading my comments and taking the time to give a detailed reply, I appreciate you doing so. :) 

I'll preface my reply by saying that while I was writing up an issue related to this for Homebrew I did gain some clarity with regard to the more common non-Ansible use case.

In the interests of enabling you to prioritize your time I'll now skip to my conclusion and you can read my "working" & other thoughts after that, if it is still of interest...

### In conclusion

My suggestion for where to go from here:

 1. Definitely a documentation change. (Which I now feel I'm in an improved position to write due to my clearer understanding of how the different pieces fit together.)
 2. I would still like to advocate for, say, a command-line option on creation of a virtualenv that would enable "auto-activation" of the virtualenv via `sitecustomize.py` (when the virtualenv's Python interpreter binary is executed directly) for those who are looking for a wrapper script and/or using an "application" that is implemented like Ansible.
 3. If (2) is not an option, then I'd like the documentation to at least point to a suitable `sitecustomize.py` based on the approach shown in #1081. (Actually, on reflection, I guess it would be more prudent to not implement (2) until such a `sitecustomize.py` has seen some extended use--and it seems like Homebrew-installed Ansible, at a minimum, needs it, so that could be a starting point. But at some point it would be nice if the script could at least be distributed as part of `virtualenv`.)

And, if nothing else, hopefully our conversation will be helpful to others if someone else starts down this same road as confused as I was. :)

Thanks again for your time.

----

[By now I have no idea about the most logical flow of the rest of this, so you might want to skip to the "Clarification in regard to your initial reply" and later sections and then return here.]

### My improved understanding around the non-Ansible use case

As prefaced above, I can now see how in a "typical" "application" installation scenario, one wouldn't want the fact that a particular application is written in Python to affect the execution of an arbitrary script by that application that also happened to be written in Python.

Say I have an installed tool `every-ten-minutes` (that happens to be written in Python and has a `virtualenv`-based shebang line) which takes an executable path and runs that executable every ten minutes. Then, let's say, I happen to write a Python script called `print-hello` (with an `env`-based shebang line) that prints "hello" to the console and I want to supply that as the executable to `every-ten-minutes` (i.e. `every-ten-minutes print-hello`). I see the logic in not setting the `PATH` for `print-hello` in this situation because clearly it may depend on default/system Python libraries not available within the `every-ten-minutes` virtualenv.

And, it would seem that this is the most typical use case for a "from the outside" use of a virtualenv.


#### Is "lack of consistent behavior" a bug?

*But* if I try to execute `every-ten-minutes print-hello` from *within* an activated virtualenv there's no guarantee I'll get the same result because the child process will inherit the modified `PATH`.

 * Is this behavior intentional?
 * Is the difference in behavior intentional?
 * Is it actually a bug that should be fixed by ensuring the child process receives the *original unmodified* `PATH` instead? That way both forms of virtualenv use would behave in the same way.

Note: I'm not *actually* advocating changing to use the original `PATH` for child processes of an activated virtualenv Python. If people haven't been clamoring for this to be changed I assume it's not a big deal.

*But* I think it does show that it's not entirely unreasonable to anticipate that the same behavior might occur with a "from the outside use" as well.

It does all make me wonder whether people generally just don't use the same environment alongside Python scripts from both within and without. (Although requests for wrapper scripts suggest that it *does* happen sometimes.)


#### The virtualenv + Ansible + inventory scripts (+ Homebrew) edge case

So, it would appear that virtualenv + Ansible + inventory scripts (+ Homebrew) now just happens to be a really gnarly edge-case that's making multiple abstractions leak or something. :) (In Homebrew terms it seems a little like an "application" which *also* supplies its own "bindings".)

Somewhat amusingly, [Homebrew documentation](https://docs.brew.sh/Python-for-Formula-Authors.html) specifically uses Ansible as an example when it says:

> Homebrew draws a distinction between Python **applications** and Python **libraries**. The difference is that users generally do not care that applications are written in Python; *it is unusual that a user would expect to be able to `import foo` after installing an application*. Examples of applications are `ansible` and `jrnl`.

:D *sigh*

However it seems this approach is intended to stick around, according to <https://github.com/ansible/ansible/issues/17147#issuecomment-241029301>: "We plan to make more use of the ansible libraries in the dynamic inventory scripts over time" 


### Clarification in regard to your initial reply 

> > "I'd expect a "virtual environment" to replicate the same experience as installing the application directly in the global non-virtual environment."
> 
> you've missed an important point - a non-activated virtualenv is not on PATH and behaves exactly as the system Python would if that weren't on PATH either. It's just that it's a lot rarer for anyone to run the system Python without it being on PATH.
> 

Apologies, I believe I did actually understand your point originally, and had intended *that* sentence to be read unaltered as "I'd expect [an activated] 'virtual environment' to replicate the same experience as installing the application directly in the global non-virtual environment."

But I think my "...on re-reading this, the issue is not about what happens in a virtual environment..." remark may have confused things a little. The "this" I was referring to applied to the bullet points following the statement, not the paragraphs proceeding it--so my remark was intended to be read as "...on re-reading [the following bullet points], the issue is not about what happens in a virtual environment...". (Writing non-linearly is hard...)


### Defining terms used

> > * Your perspective seems to be that [...snip...].
> > * My perspective is that in a global installation a subprocess Python would use the same Python as its parent and so in a virtual environment the subprocess Python should also use the same Python as its parent.
> > 
> 
> This is the first point where I think your view is going astray. There's no such thing as "a subprocess Python".

Alas, my use of the phrase "a subprocess Python" was *intended* to be short-hand to refer to "the Python interpreter binary which was first found on the `PATH` by the `env` tool and executed as the newly created child process created as a result of the call to a function of the `subprocess` module" but apparently I need to define my terms more clearly. :)

So I believe my understanding of this bit does match yours.

> I don't know if I'm explaining things you already know - if so, I apologise

Graciously accepted but no need for an apology, it made sense to clarify that I understood--after all, you had no way of knowing that I hadn't included *every* background detail I knew, as much as it might've seemed the case by volume of text. :D

> The Python program calling subprocess could modify the PATH environment variable if it chose to, but the Python language certainly shouldn't do so without being asked

It's possibly a moot point but I'd see value in drawing a distinction between "the Python language" (by which I assume you mean the Python interpreter binary) and "the Python site configuration" in this case. I'd agree the language wouldn't be expected to have modified the `PATH` but in a special circumstance I think it wouldn't be unreasonable that it might be possible for "the Python site configuration" to have modified the `PATH`. (Of course, given #1081 I'm obviously biased in this regard. :) ) 


#### Background (I): The distinction between "use" and "activate" 

It seems to me that some of the confusion around talking about virtualenv is due to the project drawing a distinction between "using a virtualenv" versus "activating and then using a virtualenv". Coming from outside the project and being unaware of virtualenv's implementation details, the existence of and need for this distinction is not obvious.

(Side note: I'm quoting out of context but this quote I found earlier while closing some tabs seems to intersect with this aspect: "Using the virtualenv's python interpreter is how you use a virtualenv." via <https://github.com/pypa/virtualenv/issues/124#issuecomment-1040000>)


#### Background (II): "Intention" versus "Implementation"

I think another factor leading to confusion is lack of shared understanding around "intention" versus "implementation detail". (Which also relates to the lack of awareness about "use" vs "activation".)

For example, I had assumed that because the `PATH` value is modified when one "uses" (an activated virtualenv) that this `PATH` modification was considered an "intentional" "part" of the virtualenv but it seems to turn out this is just an "implementation detail" related to how the correct Python binary is found.

Another example is that I assumed that the split between modifications made in the activate script and modifications made in `site.py` was an "implementation detail" but as I understand it is "intentional" that only the modifications made in `site.py` are considered part of virtualenv "use".

Additionally, I assumed that the need to manually "activate" a virtualenv was an "implementation detail" related to there being no easy way to perform the task automatically when in a shell, rather than an "intentional" distinction between "activation" and "use".


#### Background (III): Defining the "Encapsulation" and "Isolation" use cases

Broadly, my impression is that the two primary use cases for virtualenv are:

 * "Passively" using a virtualenv "from the outside"  (i.e. not activated) for purposes of encapsulation.

 * "Actively" using a virtualenv "from the inside" (i.e. activated) for purposes of isolation (from "sibling" installs, shall we say).

It seems where a use case "crosses the boundaries" of these two categories is where issues occur, e.g.:

 * The Ansible inventory script use case. (Needs "auto activation".)
 * The cronjob/web-server use case. (Needs a "wrapper" script.) 


### Mental models revisited

> > As I understand it, when an virtual environment is "activated":
> > 
> > - The correct Python library search paths for the virtual environment are set (via sys.path); and,
> > - The PATH (and other environment variables) are modified to ensure the correct Python binary (i.e. the one within the virtual environment) is used.
> > 
> 
> Not correct.
> 
> Specifically activation doesn't do (1) at all. The standard Python executable sets sys.path and it does so based on various things (the location of the executable). The site.py file shipped with virtualenv does further things to modify this. But none of this is dependent on activation - it happens for all virtualenvs.
> 
> What activation does is (1) add the bin directory of the virtualenv to the front of $PATH, and (2) [...snip...]
> 
> So, the specific effect of all this is that the only practical difference activating a virtual environment has is in how PATH is set.
> 

Essentially the fundamental mismatch between our mental models of virtualenv seems to come down to the impact of the "use" vs "activation" and "intention" vs "implementation" factors as evidenced in the above quoted section.

So, it's definitely helpful to see it spelled out like that.

[Quote moved from here to next section]

> In my view, the real confusion here comes from the term "activating".

I would agree it's a significant contributing factor. (per "Background (I)")

> It sounds like it's a really clever bit of machinery, but it isn't - it's just adding the virtualenv to PATH.

While the specific implementation details might be straightforward, I feel like the task it is performing ("ensure the correct virtualenv Python binary is selected") is actually a significant part of virtualenv's entire functionality.


### Resolution for the Ansible + virtualenv (+ Homebrew) use case

> Whether your scripts are written to assume that python is a command available on PATH, and it points to the "correct" Python, is a matter for those scripts to get right. It sounds like the Ansible scripts you're referring to do make that assumption, but the Homebrew approach of "using a virtual environment" is somehow violating that assumption if you don't activate the virtualenv.
>
> That's an issue that will need to be resolved between those two parties - it's not something that virtualenv can (or should, in my opinion) "fix".

While Homebrew can definitely be *a* location for a "fix", I do feel there is a wider, more general Ansible + virtualenv issue--after all, installation via Homebrew isn't the only scenario in which Ansible ends up in a virtualenv.

And, Ansible *does* successfully work in either a global installation or a manually activated virtualenv, so I suspect they might have minimal motivation to make any changes. (Although maybe "installing" inventory scripts or shipping the `contrib` files are options.)

Given that there are other actual scenarios (activation via wrapper script) and potential scenarios (an application that acts in the same manner as Ansible) that are/can be also affected I feel like the common element is virtualenv. Which suggests it might be a good place for a "fix" (or, at least, to store a "fix" :) ).

Perhaps, even if the need for a "fix" is based on a fundamental misunderstanding of how virtualenv is intended to be used and is implemented. :D  

> The resolution may be as simple as saying "running the Homebrew scripts without activating the virtualenv is not supported" or it may be something complex like patching the scripts - I can't say.

I'm certainly hoping it's as simple as dropping a `sitecustomize.py` into `site-packages` on installation when needed but I accept that might not be desirable as far as the virtualenv project is concerned. :)

Once again, thanks for your time, reading this far and your work on the project!

How to handle reductions (e.g. `mean`, `sum`, `prod`, `std`) in NE3 remains an open question.  In NE2 they could only be performed as the last operation, and then they were only single-threaded.  Due to these limitations they did not contribute a lot of value to the library, in my opinion.
For reductions to be of significance to NE3 we should have the ability to perform them at any point (especially because NE3 supports multi-line operations) and thread them efficiently.  
Currently the pre-allocation of the output array via broadcasting is fairly fragile.  For reductions, a pre-requisite would be to verify the broadcasting through the entire program in-advance.  This is likely necessary to perform in C inside `NumExpr_run` through the NumPy API because calls to `d np.broadcast` generate new Python objects and hence are quite expensive (~ 8 us per call).  The actual C-code that `np.broadcast` uses is relatively simple and could be copied directly.

You are correct in saying that `-DBROTLI_ENCODER_CLEANUP_ON_OOM` works around the exit. However, compression still fails and `BrotliEncoderCompressStream()` returns `BROTLI_FALSE`.

Here is a solution I found to pass my tests: _Don't_ call `BROTLI_ALLOC` if `literals_count` is `0`. It's a small change to https://github.com/google/brotli/blob/master/enc/block_splitter.c#L137, just replace 

```C
uint8_t* literals = BROTLI_ALLOC(m, uint8_t, literals_count);
```

with

```C
uint8_t* literals = literals_count ? BROTLI_ALLOC(m, uint8_t, literals_count) : NULL;
```

There're two separate issues here:

1. The warning you're seeing is asking you to provide a custom `user_agent` value to the Nominatim instance creation. The warning also contains the code sample which fixes that: `Nominatim(user_agent="my-application")`. See #314 for rationale. 
Could you please point at the exact place in the docs where you saw a Nominatim instance creation without `user_agent` (which causes the warning)? The latest docs at https://geopy.readthedocs.io/ shouldn't have such.

2. The exception (and the related traceback) is caused by TLS verification problems. There's a lot of possible causes for that. All of them are not related to geopy, unless you have specified a custom invalid ssl context by using `geopy.geocoders.options.default_ssl_context` or `Nominatim(ssl_context=...)`. 
Make sure that https://nominatim.openstreetmap.org/ can be opened in browser (if not – debug that first). 
You could also try to use a CA bundle from `certifi` instead of the system-provided one. There's a code sample for that in the docs for `geopy.geocoders.options.default_ssl_context` at  https://geopy.readthedocs.io/en/stable/#geopy.geocoders.options

P.S. Please consider asking such questions on Stack Overflow next time. GitHub issues are for tracking issues (e.g. bugs and feature requests), not for usage questions and support. - Check if you're using the <23.5 sample that is available on the GREAT3 site: if yes, then allow use of ```deep=True```.
- Check if you're using the <25.2 sample that is available on the GREAT3 site: if yes, then forbid use of ```deep=True```.

It doesn't allow the possibility that for user-defined samples, one might want to use ```deep=True```.  We should probably allow that, while documenting (in the docstring) that it's only meant for a particular case and won't do what the user intends for a completely arbitrary case.

(disclaimer: I'm not in charge of making icons)

The workflow should the following:
* Prepare your development machine (you will need ruby, less and jekyll)
* Clone the master branch
* Edit the .otf file here: https://github.com/FortAwesome/Font-Awesome/blob/master/src/assets/font-awesome/fonts/FontAwesome.otf (take a look at this guide: https://github.com/blog/1135-the-making-of-octicons)
* Edit icons.yml here: https://github.com/FortAwesome/Font-Awesome/blob/master/src/icons.yml
* Upload the otf at fontsquirrel to generate the webfont: http://www.fontsquirrel.com/tools/webfont-generator
* Regenerate less and scss and css via `jekyll build` (do not edit `/less` and `/scss` folders, they are generated)


Hope this helps

1. `pip_accel.caches.s3.PatchedBotoConfig.__init__()` imports `ConfigParser` from the `boto.pyami.config` module.

from ConfigParser import SafeConfigParser as ConfigParser

# Migration guide

The following regular expressions can help you adapt your OpenFisca country package to the new version of core.

## Replacing variables `stop_date` attribute by `end`

Throughout your code:

Replace

```
stop_date = date\((\d{4}), (\d{1}), (\d{1})\)
```

by 

```
end = '\1-0\2-0\3'
```

Replace

```
stop_date = date\((\d{4}), (\d{2}), (\d{1})\)
```

by 

```
end = '\1-\2-0\3'
```

Replace

```
stop_date = date\((\d{4}), (\d{1}), (\d{1})\)
```

by 

```
end = '\1-0\2-0\3'
```

Replace

```
stop_date = date\((\d{4}), (\d{1}), (\d{2})\)
```

by 

```
end = '\1-0\2-\3'
```

Replace

```
stop_date = date\((\d{4}), (\d{2}), (\d{2})\)
```

by 

```
end = '\1-\2-\3'
```

All sizes seem correct when compared to system.getsizeof, one exception the size of the __slots__ in the Foo() instance.  The additional 8 bytes (compared to the size of SLOTS) is due to the following assumption inside function asizeof._dir2 ...

            # assume __slots__ tuple/list
            # is holding the attr values

The instance specific __slots__ values are assumed to be stored in a tuple.  The instance must hold the reference (i.e. pointer) to that tuple, the additional 8 bytes (for 64-bit).

Perhaps that assumption is incorrect and should be revisited and verified against the C implementation of __slots__-based instances.  But the difference will at be 4 or 8 bytes or a at most 2-3%.

Keep in mind, the ‘a’ in ‘asizeof’, ‘asized’, etc. means approximate, not accurate ;).  Hope this makes sense

So a couple of concerns:
1. "local -r" doesn't work with /bin/sh on Debian (may also be a problem on other distros, but I didn't check all of them)
2. On Debian (and other distros that support SELinux), there is the following comment in /etc/pam.d/sshd:
# SELinux needs to be the first session rule.  This ensures that any
# lingering context has been cleared.  Without this it is possible that a
# module could execute code in the wrong domain.

This change adds the oslogin session rule before that, which doesn't seem to be where we would want it.

If you find bugs with `lambda` and maps derived from `ppft`, then please open a new ticket.  I know there are some bugs when it comes to locating the correct lambda 100% of the time from buffer memory.

`pathos` intentionally keeps the pool instance alive, so the typical `close` then `join` doesn't destroy the pool, it leaves a joined pool in memory.  This is intentional, because it's faster to restart from an existing pool than it is to create a new one.

If you want to reuse an pool after you have called `close`/`join`, then you can call `restart`.  If you want to delete the pool (i.e. clear it from memory), then call `clear`.

@ttulka I do not recommend this at all and AutoPublish alias is not meant to be used for this case. 

Reason why I strongly encourage teams not to do this:
You are changing your prod Lambda for a dev environment. Which means you are actually changing the same resource that is taking Production traffic. In the event something goes wrong, you now have an outage. There are other reasons you would want to avoid this but this is the biggest one to me. 

To reduce this concern and limit blast radius for a given deployment/scoping your functions to each stage, you can use multiple stacks. In this model, you still maintain your one CloudFormation template but instead of dev and prod updating the same CloudFormation stack, dev would update the dev stack (with the correct parameters) and prod would update the prod stack (with the correct prod parameters). Since you are in one account, you will have to update the function name to be scoped to dev or prod as well. You can do this through, appending 'dev' or 'prod' to the function name, passing this value through another Parameter, or just letting CloudFormation do it's thing and have it control the names of your resources.

Does this model make sense to use for your case?

I had a scheme to make the header exchange work, but I do need to confirm one thing before I know if it works.

The idea is that authors would provide ``affected_{usernames,packages}`` accessors (along with a few other settings for a message, like importance) and we could add something in the library to use those to create workable headers.

For example, suppose the ``affected_packages`` is ``['kernel', 'kernel-headers', 'kernel-tools']``, we take that and add the following headers:
```
{
    'fedora_messaging_packages_kernel': True,
    'fedora_messaging_packages_kernel_headers': True,
    'fedora_messaging_packages_kernel_tools': True,
}
```
A user can then specify they want any messages that reference one of a list of packages, and we turn that into a header binding for any of those packages prefixed with 'fedora_messaging_packages'. Same deal with users.

One thing I'm not sure about is if "any messages that are about the kernel package AND importance=high" will work. Does the ALL header binding require the headers to match exactly, or can the ones I specify be a subset of the total headers? If that doesn't work we will need a shim service to transform the message topic/headers and republish them before notifications handles them.

As of `v0.3.0` (Mar 8), configuration via `.dwrc` is deprecated in favor of `dwave.conf`.

[Introduction](http://dwave-cloud-client.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/intro.html) to the new config system is available in the docs. Detailed docs on how to use it under [`dwave.cloud.client.Client.from_config`](http://dwave-cloud-client.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/generated/dwave.cloud.client.Client.from_config.html#dwave.cloud.client.Client.from_config).

Now there are several ways/layers on which you can define the default solver: via config file - under solver section and under defaults section, via env var, and during call to `from_config` via kwargs.

Lastly, feature-based solver selection (i.e. not name/id-based) is also available in `v0.4.3+`. It's not yet visible on RTD, but it's [documented in docstring](https://github.com/dwavesystems/dwave-cloud-client/blob/e1af7628278858229012af59805ef9f52d21ba13/dwave/cloud/client.py#L608).

You can, for example, get the first QPU-based solver with at least 1000 qubits (but no upper bound) like this:
```
first_qpu_solver = client.solvers(qpu=True, num_qubits=[1000, None])[0]
```

> On Mar 1, 2016, at 5:14 PM, Adrien Tétar <notifications@github.com> wrote:
> 
>> Switch all libraries away from robofab and to defcon and ufolib.
> 
> I don't think spec makers and friends(1) will ditch robofab, but we can work with that I think most of the time, libraries aren't very robofab-dependent.

RoboFab has historically been two things:

1. A basic read/write lib for UFOs: ufoLib, glifLib.
2. A scripting API for working inside of a font editing environment. (That can be in a UI or raw object interaction.) glyph = font["A"]

The read/write portion is moving out. I merged the read/write functionality into a submodule years ago and that is now the ufoLib in the official UFO spec library.

The scripting API is the hard part. The current API was cobbled together over a decade ago when we were trying to move into FontLab. The idea of the common API was script portability. For example, when I switched to RoboFont, my scripts from FontLab just worked. I didn't have to rewrite them for a new object model. That has been a HUGE success and there is no way that we're deprecating it. I can't even imagine how many designers use that API in their daily workflow.

What does need to be done with the scripting part is that it needs to be updated to support UFO 3. The existing code is not going to work for that. It's brittle and filled with environment hacks. Our plan is to turn RoboFab into an API documentation and a subclassable base implementation (built on top of defcon). The environment specific implementations (RoboFont, Glyphs, FontLab, etc.) will be up to the developers of the environments. RoboFab will have a way for those environments to  to register their implementations on the fly.

>> Deprecate robofab and don't update it anymore and advise everyone to move away from it.
> 
> The second point has pretty much happened for quite a while now unless I'm missing something. 
> For the rest robofab is what most people in the type scene know and use (it's been around for quite a while, too) and they have scripts and things written with it so asking for deprecation/move away is rather unrealistic, I reckon (it's like asking programmers to stop doing C because there's something new that's better, not gonna happen). Also there are some user-facing changes to going from robofab to defcon mostly robofab has some more abstractions (I don't mean to say that robofab is necessarily easier to use because of it, it just has some differences in its APIs), the difference isn't huge but sufficient to be taken into account when talking deprecation.

Right. RoboFab was designed to be a scripting API. We needed to build some apps and RoboFab was available so we bolted app stuff into it. We quickly realized that app stuff was not very fast. So, I built defcon. Defcon was designed to be a fast base layer with inter-object communication, managed caching and other stuff for apps. It's not a friendly scripting library. It shouldn't be that.

>> Old versions of libraries that support UFO 2 can live in a branch forever.

I agree with this. It's the eventual goal.

> So in my experience people won't migrate to ufo3 unless you give them a reason to. There are definitely reasons from a developer standpoint to do so just like there are reasons to go from Python 2 to Python 3, that doesn't mean people necessarily feel compelled to do the upgrade. FWIW.

RoboFab's API not supporting UFO 3 has been a huge reason for the slow adoption. Most of my libraries have ufo3 branches that are ready.

I absolutely see what you mean about dependency hell though. It's all over the place. I think a good thing to do would be to establish some dependency guidelines or at least chart out what depends on what. That would help us try to get rid of some of the circularity.

If we can get RoboFab + ufoLb resolved I think that would allow the UFO 3 implementations to become the master branches and the current masters could move to a legacy branch. I really want to get the RoboFab stuff done. I just don't have the coding time that I used to. If you want to use a team to get this done, let's draft a plan. (I have more detailed plans for RoboFab somewhere and some volunteers. I've been the problem.) That's probably going to be the fastest way.

It is important that the docstring of the function calculator aligns with the calculated value. The name can be ambiguous, as long as the docstring is congruent. 

The docstring that is contained in mean_autocorrelation contains the following definition of the mean autocorrelation:
![bildschirmfoto 2017-07-05 um 16 22 29](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/13162426/27868796-27949fe8-619e-11e7-91cf-e2c1e8f3939d.png)

if I calculate that by hand for the example `x=[1, 2, 3]`,  I get

``` python
expected_res = 1 / 3 * 1 / np.var(x) * ( 1/(3-1) * ((1-0)*(2-0)+(2-0)*(-3-0) + 1/(3-2) * ((1-0)*(-3-0)))
expected_res = 1 / 3 * 1 / np.var(x) * ( 1/2 * (2-6) + -3)
expected_res =  -5/ 3 * 1 / np.var(x) # is -0.35714285714285715

# but
np.nanmean(acf(x, unbiased=True)[1:]) # is  -0.5357142857142857
np.nanmean(acf(x)[1:]) # is -0.25
# tsfresh is -0.589285714286
```
So, none of the presented ways to calculate the mean autocorrelation is as described in the docstring.

I think the problem here is that it is not clear over what are we averaging the autocorrelation? lags or summands in the most inner sum?

actually, if we replace the outer correction factor `1/n` by `1/(n-1)`, then `np.nanmean(acf(x, unbiased=True)[1:])`and docstring are equal.

Next steps:

- From the look of it, I would be in favor to use `statsmodels.tsa.stattools.acf` with FFT due to its speed. Can you create a small plot where you inspect the different ways for different time series lengths?
- I would be in favor to change the docstring (1/n to 1/n-1) and use `np.nanmean(acf(x, unbiased=True)[1:])`

The fix is that the conda-env post-link script has been removed.  It has been replaced by a modified Lib/site.py file in each Python installation, plus a library_bin.pth file in each Python installation.  The combination of these lead to Library/bin being added to os.environ["PATH"] for any Python process.  Any programs you install that end up in Library/bin (for example, patch) will still not be on PATH for older installations.  You can either modify PATH to add Library/bin yourself, or uninstall and reinstall using a new installer (Anaconda >= 2.4.0; Miniconda >= 3.18)

That's quite funny actually (I know this is generally not an ideal way of doing this but serves as a good example of how things can go south):
```sh
    if [ -n "$DEFAULT_CONDA_ENV" ]; then
        echo "Can only install Ruby in the root conda environment."
        exit 1
    fi
    ROOT_PREFIX=`python -c "import os; from conda import config; \
        print os.path.realpath(config.root_dir)"`
    if [ "$PREFIX" != "$ROOT_PREFIX" ]; then
        echo "Can only install Ruby in the root conda environment."
        exit 1
    fi
```
So, conda happened to be unlinked while this pre-link was running because it wanted to update itself, so pre-link for ruby failed, which in turn made the WHOLE linking stage to fail, so conda never got to linking itself back in.

If a pre-link for a certain package fails, should the whole linking stage fail? If the package for which -pre-link failed has been previously unlinked, should conda attempt to link the old version back?

More to the point of the issue, yes, this is expected behavior.  In preparation for https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/3912, when the base environment by default will be empty (no python or anything else) we've decoupled the base environment python version from the python version of newly-created environments.

If you'd like to keep the old behavior, you can do

    conda config --system --add pinned_packages python=2

Note that we still have hard-coded conda *not* change python major-minor versions in an existing environment, if the change isn't explicitly requested.

My main use for `conda search --reverse-dependency` was to determine what downstream packages depend on a certain package.  For that purpose, searches would be by name only without a version or build.  

For example, if I wanted to see what packages require networkx and might be affected by an update:

```
~$ conda search --reverse-dependency scipy
Fetching package metadata ...........
accelerate                   2.0                np110py27_p0  defaults        
                             2.0                np110py34_p0  defaults        
                             2.0                np110py35_p0  defaults        
                             2.0                 np19py27_p0  defaults        
                             2.0                 np19py34_p0  defaults        
                             2.0                 np19py35_p0  defaults        
                             2.0.1              np110py27_p0  defaults        
                             2.0.1              np110py34_p0  defaults        
                             2.0.1              np110py35_p0  defaults
...
```
Or I want to know why automat is in the repository (because twisted requires it):

```
~$ conda search --reverse-dependency automat
Fetching package metadata ...........
twisted                      17.1.0                   py27_0  defaults        
                             17.1.0                   py35_0  defaults        
                             17.1.0                   py36_0  defaults        
                             17.5.0                   py27_0  defaults        
                             17.5.0                   py35_0  defaults        
                             17.5.0                   py36_0  defaults        
                             17.9.0           py36hb8d8d5a_0  defaults        
                             17.9.0           py27h7008fb7_0  defaults        
                             17.9.0           py35h812867c_0  defaults  
```

At least for me my expectation is that conda search --reverse-dependecy would compare each requirement of every package in all currently enabled chanel against the provided matchspec and return the packages where any of the requirements were met by the matchspec.  

The most common case would be to find all packages which depend on package foo or bar, but I can imagine there may be cases where a including a version or build might be helpful.

This is worth considering.  The [un]alias is atomic in the end, but the selection criteria would be exceptionally tricky to implement at the command-line.

How do you select the indices to remove, and add from the alias?  It would almost necessitate a 2 level index selection: one for the add, and one for the remove.  This could make for a fairly long command-line.

You're using the `--disk-space` flag. That's why this is getting triggered.  You have too many indices for the query for the delete by space, and I did not apply the logic to prevent over-sized lists of indices to that method.

Here's the offending line:

https://github.com/elastic/curator/blob/master/curator/api/filter.py#L337

It will need to be updated to batch the results in smaller chunks, then re-assemble them into a single array again.  I don't like having to add this (but I will to keep it from breaking) because it suggests a massive-scale usage of delete by space, which is NotGood™, for a variety of reasons, including the ones quoted below.  Please see: http://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/client/curator/current/delete.html#_deleting_indices_by_space

>**Deleting Indices By Space**
>
>This option is for those who want to retain indices based on disk consumption, rather than by a set number of days. There are some important caveats surrounding this choice.
>
>**Caveats**
>
>* Elasticsearch cannot calculate the size of closed indices. Elasticsearch does not keep tabs on how much disk-space closed indices consume. If you close indices, your space calculations will be inaccurate.
>* Indices consume resources just by existing. You could run into performance and/or operational snags in Elasticsearch as the count of indices climbs.
>* You need to manually calculate how much space across all nodes. The total you give will be the sum of all space consumed across all nodes in your cluster. If you use shard allocation to put more shards or indices on a single node, it will not affect the total space reported by the cluster, but you may still run out of space on that node.
>
>These are only a few of the caveats. This is still a valid use-case, especially for those running a single-node test box, however, so we include this option for your convenience.

Even if you change your shard allocation per node to be by space, you have no idea which indices will keep what shards on what nodes.  Deleting the "oldest" indices could free up space on 2 nodes, but leave several others completely full, which could result in a lot of extra shard reallocation just to balance the cluster.  

It is much wiser to use monitoring software to see what size _n_ days worth of indices can accommodate and then keep that many indices by time, rather than by space.

You also are feeding a list of both your `stage` and `production` indices to be acted on.  Delete by space will simply feed the indices alphabetically (or reverse, if that flag is chosen), as it comes after the other filters.  This means you could potentially delete entire indices you don't want to delete, and not free the space you actually care about.

While I will fix this, because I don't like the potential of this bug to keep coming up, the fact that you are hitting it is indicative of a potentially dangerous approach to index management.

Did you see the hubshare spec we wrote?

Sent from my iPhone

> On Jan 15, 2017, at 2:03 PM, Jessica B. Hamrick <notifications@github.com> wrote:
> 
> I would eventually like to replace the nbgrader exchange directory with a more robust solution, namely, a JupyterHub "exchange" service that manages released assignments, submissions, and feedback. This has the drawback that people won't be able to use nbgrader's file management capabilities unless they are using JupyterHub. In practice, I don't think anyone is using nbgrader's file management capabilities without JupyterHub anyway, though (but if I am wrong about this, someone should correct me!). Here's how I will imagine this working.
> 
> @lgpage @minrk @ellisonbg @willingc @dsblank I would appreciate any feedback you have on this proposal!
> 
> Permissions
> 
> All authentication will be handled by JupyterHub, which will tell the service who the current user is and what group(s) they are a part of. The exchange service will handle any number of courses on the same machine, and for each course, will require that there are two groups specified: one for instructors (who are allowed to release, fetch, submit, and collect assignments and return feedback) and one for students (who are allowed to fetch and submit assignments and download feedback). This would be configured something like this:
> 
> c.ExchangeApp.groups = {
>     'course1': dict(instructors='instructors_course1', students='students_course1'),
>     'course2': dict(instructors='instructors_course2', students='students_course2'),
>     ...
> }
> API
> 
> The exchange service will define a REST api that the nbgrader commands (release, fetch, submit, collect, etc.) can access.
> 
> /api/assignments
> 
> GET /api/assignments/<course_id> -- list all assignments for a course (students+instructors)
> /api/assignment
> 
> GET /api/assignment/<course_id>/<assignment_id> -- download a copy of an assignment (students+instructors)
> POST /api/assignment/<course_id>/<assignment_id> -- release an assignment (instructors only)
> /api/submissions
> 
> GET /api/submissions/<course_id>/<assignment_id> -- list all submissions for an assignment from all students (instructors only)
> GET /api/submissions/<course_id>/<assignment_id>/<student_id> -- list all submissions for an assignment from a particular student (instructors+students, though students are restricted to only viewing their own submissions)
> /api/submission
> 
> POST /api/submission/<course_id>/<assignment_id>/<student_id> -- submit a copy of an assignment (students+instructors)
> GET /api/submission/<course_id>/<assignment_id>/<student_id> -- download a student's submitted assignment (instructors only)
> /api/feedback
> 
> POST /api/feedback/<course_id>/<assignment_id>/<student_id> -- upload feedback on a student's assignment (instructors only)
> GET /api/feedback/<course_id>/<assignment_id>/<student_id> -- download feedback on a student's assignment (instructors+students, though students are restricted to only viewing their own feedback)
> Exchange implementation
> 
> Under the hood, the exchange service will continue to store files directly on the filesystem, but they will all have the same permissions (read and write only for the user running the exchange service). I think this is a better option that doing it with a database because we don't really need any fancy relational features here and this also makes it easier for instructors to inspect files in the exchange manually. If someone feels strongly that a database should be used then I might be able to be convinced otherwise, though.
> 
> Regardless, I do want to implement some form of checksumming, though, because I have noticed at least in the current implementation that sometimes if the system is under heavy load that the submissions are occasionally incomplete or corrupted (e.g. missing timestamp.txt or something).
> 
> Existing nbgrader apps
> 
> The existing nbgrader apps will be reworked to make requests to the exchange API rather than copying to and from the exchange directory.
> 
> One thing I am not quite sure of is how the command line apps get properly authenticated, because the authentication is normally happening in the browser, not the command line. I see two possible solutions:
> 
> One solution to this is to say that these commands can only be used through the server extension, and then have that extension pass the authentication information to the command line apps. This is probably the easiest but then it means you can't just run the commands from the command line anymore.
> The other solution is to require some how that users re-authenticate from the command line. I am not really sure how to this in a general way that handles all the forms of authentication that JupyterHub uses. Maybe @minrk can weigh in on the feasibility of this, but from what I know about how this works it doesn't seem like a particularly feasible option to me?

I also ran into this issue as I prefer to render matplotlib images and import them into the notebook with `` tags. There is a solution to adjust the caching. In this case the server is set-up to prevent caching for files with arguments to their http request. In notebook.files.handlers.py you can make the edit: ```python if self.get_argument("download", False): self.set_header('Content-Disposition','attachment; filename="%s"' % name) if "?" in name: self.set_header("Cache-Control", "no-cache; no-store") # get mimetype from filename ``` now access images which you want to force refreshing with as `!["title"](image.png?arg)` or `` and the webserver will prevent caching. In general, the browser should prevent caching if you vary the arguments anyway, but I was finding that firefox at least would still cache them, so I added this fix. I was actually going to submit my own bug report to suggest the addition, but here is a good place. If this is a reasonable change, It would be nice to insert it to the project.

@jdfreder The issue is that users have been using `d,d` to delete cells up until now, and if they encounter a case where there is a marked cell that is different from the selected cell, users are going to be surprised. Personally,

> Deletes the selected cell, unless there are marked cells somewhere on the page, even if out of view.

is very unintuitive to me. The cells that are being operated on are the ones I'm looking at. Since the few follows the selection around (not the marked cells), my first assumption would be that things like deleting cells operates on the one that I have selected, *especially* if the marked cells aren't even visible.

To address the issue for now, I think the following two things will be sufficient:

1. Always unmark cells after an operation (e.g. merge, split). Users aren't going to know how to unmark cells themselves (some will figure it out, many probably won't) so we shouldn't put them in a situation that will be confusing for them in the first place.
2. (@Carreau 's suggestion) If, in the chance there is a marked cell somewhere, make sure that surprising behavior can't happen. Any destructive operations (merge, split, delete, etc.) should be disabled if the selected cell is not also a marked cell.

> So,if I want to use 121 seconds to do my job,and I set the msg-message="120s".The nsqd also force to requeue the message,does it mean I should set the msg-message>121 seconds?

Yes.  The argument to pass to to `nsq.Reader()` is `msg_timeout`.

> By the way,do i use the message.enable_async() in a correct way?

Yes, except for where you're invoking `finish()`. In general it's not safe to a call a method that interacts with a Tornado IO loop (as `finish()` does) from a thread other the thread that IO loop is running on (in this case, the thread the `Reader` is running on).

You don't want to use Python threads directly, but rather via a tornado executor facilities.  An example is [here](https://gist.github.com/methane/2185380).  You'll want to invoke `finish()` in the callback you pass to `run_background()`.

.. notice for github users

Disclaimer
==========

This is a **DEVELOPER PREVIEW** intended for developers and testers only.

**DO NOT RUN THIS CODE ON A PRODUCTION SERVER. IT WILL INSTALL CERTIFICATES
SIGNED BY A TEST CA, AND WILL CAUSE CERT WARNINGS FOR USERS.**

Browser-trusted certificates will be available in the coming months.

For more information regarding the status of the project, please see
https://letsencrypt.org. Be sure to checkout the
`Frequently Asked Questions (FAQ) <https://community.letsencrypt.org/t/frequently-asked-question
s-faq/26#topic-title>`_.

About the Let's Encrypt Client
==============================

|build-status| |coverage| |docs| |container|

In short: getting and installing SSL/TLS certificates made easy (`watch demo video`_).

The Let's Encrypt Client is a tool to automatically receive and install
X.509 certificates to enable TLS on servers. The client will
interoperate with the Let's Encrypt CA which will be issuing browser-trusted
certificates for free.

It's all automated:

* The tool will prove domain control to the CA and submit a CSR (Certificate
  Signing Request).
* If domain control has been proven, a certificate will get issued and the tool
  will automatically install it.

All you need to do to sign a single domain is::

  user@www:~$ sudo letsencrypt -d www.example.org auth

For multiple domains (SAN) use::

  user@www:~$ sudo letsencrypt -d www.example.org -d example.org auth

and if you have a compatible web server (Apache or Nginx), Let's Encrypt can
not only get a new certificate, but also deploy it and configure your
server automatically!::

  user@www:~$ sudo letsencrypt -d www.example.org run


**Encrypt ALL the things!**


.. |build-status| image:: https://travis-ci.org/letsencrypt/letsencrypt.svg?branch=master
   :target: https://travis-ci.org/letsencrypt/letsencrypt
   :alt: Travis CI status

.. |coverage| image:: https://coveralls.io/repos/letsencrypt/letsencrypt/badge.svg?branch=master
   :target: https://coveralls.io/r/letsencrypt/letsencrypt
   :alt: Coverage status

.. |docs| image:: https://readthedocs.org/projects/letsencrypt/badge/
   :target: https://readthedocs.org/projects/letsencrypt/
   :alt: Documentation status

.. |container| image:: https://quay.io/repository/letsencrypt/letsencrypt/status
   :target: https://quay.io/repository/letsencrypt/letsencrypt
   :alt: Docker Repository on Quay.io

.. _`installation instructions`:
   https://letsencrypt.readthedocs.org/en/latest/using.html

.. _watch demo video: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Gas_sSB-5SU


Current Features
----------------

* Supports multiple web servers:

  - apache/2.x (tested and working on Ubuntu Linux)
  - nginx/0.8.48+ (under development)
  - standalone (runs its own simple webserver to prove you control a domain)

* The private key is generated locally on your system.
* Can talk to the Let's Encrypt (demo) CA or optionally to other ACME
  compliant services.
* Can get domain-validated (DV) certificates.
* Can revoke certificates.
* Adjustable RSA key bit-length (2048 (default), 4096, ...).
* Can optionally install a http -> https redirect, so your site effectively
  runs https only (Apache only)
* Fully automated.
* Configuration changes are logged and can be reverted.
* Text and ncurses UI.
* Free and Open Source Software, made with Python.

> That makes sense, in general, but for a timestamp like "2017-02-23T06:54:00Z", the time zone isn't ambiguous, it's UTC. The resulting datetime object really shouldn't be dependent on the system timezone

Agreed, I just meant that the reason it's happening is that `dateutil` seems to prefer to use your local zone whenever that is detected. If you change your local zone to `UTC`, `tzutc()` and `tzlocal()` are equivalent, and evidently `dateutil` prefers `tzlocal()`.

> Incidentally, it's not clear to me that #356 is a duplicate of this issue; in the snippet pasted there, it looks like the parser is returning a datetime in the wrong timezone altogether.

I think you're right that it's not a perfect duplicate. I only skimmed this one and merged all three issues in my head. I thought that it was the same underlying issue, but actually I think this one is more a priority issue (explicit call-outs should beat `tzlocal()`, even if the zones are equally appropriate) and the other one is an issue where the wrong zone is attached to a non-ambiguous datetime where datetimes are ambiguous for some portion of the year (e.g. `GMT` during Winter time could be local time or it could be UTC).

Sorry about that. Not sure there's an actual workaround for this one, though luckily it has no actual symptoms.

The method is it create a tensor as large as module parameter tensor. Then multiply the tensor with learning rate and then with the weight.

It needs another tensor, say if the parameter is 100M and then the new tensor will be 100M. It's not efficient from memory or speed.
