It's not a Theano bug: `theano.scan` has two return values, the second of which is an updates dictionary. `CustomRecurrentLayer` just discards it, while it should have been caught and passed to `theano.function` (as documented on http://deeplearning.net/software/theano/library/scan.html#using-shared-variables-gibbs-sampling). Even in the cases where compilation doesn't fail with a `MissingInputError`, it will cause each function call to use the very same dropout masks.

One way to fix this would be changing the `get_output()` API to return an update dictionary along with the output expression. Changing the API is something we should have done before Lasagne 0.1, though.
Instead of having `get_output()` return a second output, we could use `**kwargs` to pass an `updates` dictionary that is extended in a layer's `get_output_for()` if needed. Users would have to be made aware of the need for passing an `updates` dictionary (by raising an exception or a warning) if they didn't pass one but should (i.e., the recurrent layers would only need to do this when `scan` returns a non-empty `updates` dictionary).
In any case, since we need `get_output()` to handle updates, we could also use this for the `BatchNormLayer` instead of the `default_updates` trick.

To reiterate, I'd propose changing the recurrent layer's `get_output_for()` to something like:
```python
def get_output_for(self, inputs, **kwargs):
    ...
    output, updates = theano.scan(...)
    if updates:
        if not 'updates' in kwargs:
            raise ValueError("%r requires an `updates` dictionary to be "
                             "passed to `get_output()` or `get_output_for()` for "
                             "recording updates to internal Theano variables. "
                             "This needs to be passed to `theano.function()` "
                             "later on." % self)
        kwargs['updates'].update(updates)  # that one reads great, doesn't it?
    ...
```

> It's up to date as of about two weeks ago, and I can update it again if needed.

But the version you linked to still uses `Layer.get_output()`, and the `Objective` class. You should try running it without `warnings.filterwarnings` once to see which interface changes need to be incorporated to be ready for the Lasagne release.

Not a lot. It's just that if we don't support it, we can handle the distinction between `scale` and `size` it in the constructor and set `self.size` to an integer tuple in either case. I do have a condition_yaw function, but I thought it gets overwritten by the goto.

```
def condition_yaw(heading, relative=False):
    """
    Send MAV_CMD_CONDITION_YAW message to point vehicle at a specified heading (in degrees).

    This method sets an absolute heading by default, but you can set the `relative` parameter
    to `True` to set yaw relative to the current yaw heading.

    By default the yaw of the vehicle will follow the direction of travel. After setting 
    the yaw using this function there is no way to return to the default yaw "follow direction 
    of travel" behaviour (https://github.com/diydrones/ardupilot/issues/2427)

    For more information see: 
    http://copter.ardupilot.com/wiki/common-mavlink-mission-command-messages-mav_cmd/#mav_cmd_condition_yaw
    """
    if relative:
        is_relative = 1 #yaw relative to direction of travel
    else:
        is_relative = 0 #yaw is an absolute angle
    # create the CONDITION_YAW command using command_long_encode()
    msg = vehicle.message_factory.command_long_encode(
        0, 0,    # target system, target component
        mavutil.mavlink.MAV_CMD_CONDITION_YAW, #command
        0, #confirmation
        heading,    # param 1, yaw in degrees
        0,          # param 2, yaw speed deg/s
        1,          # param 3, direction -1 ccw, 1 cw
        is_relative, # param 4, relative offset 1, absolute angle 0
        0, 0, 0)    # param 5 ~ 7 not used
    # send command to vehicle
    vehicle.send_mavlink(msg)
```

Regarding the different functions - you can query the temperature on different points on the PCB:
ir_temp is measured near the IR Sensor.
mems_temp is measured near the projector.
These test points are device specific, and in theory new test points can be added for future products.

I fear that exposing temperature as an option abuses option API. 
Current API provides no way to know which options are read-only and which are read-write. Also, there is no way to know which options are safe to cache and which (like temperature) are being modified by the device. Since monitoring temperature is common use case, I would recommend adding get_temperature method.

We had an issue today and this worked, it only needs an extra change from my [PR](https://github.com/graphql-python/graphene-sqlalchemy/pull/147)

```python
@convert_sqlalchemy_type.register(types.Enum)
def convert_enum_to_enum(type, column, registry=None):
    enum_class = getattr(type, 'enum_class', None)

    if enum_class:  # Check if an enum.Enum type is used
        graphene_type = Enum.from_enum(enum_class)

    else:  # Nope, just a list of string options
        items = zip(type.enums, type.enums)
        graphene_type = Enum(type.name or "{}_{}".format(column.table, column.name), items)

    return Field(
        graphene_type,
        description=get_column_doc(column),
        required=not (is_column_nullable(column)),
    )

```

I'm strongly with Mohammad here. The current default is annoying for me and many peers. I understand the good intention of displaying always some information but falls to the fact that most boards will have more than 12 summaries.
1) With more than 12 summaries you quickly end up in the state that the first 12 (in alphabetical order) are not the interesting ones. Then you have to scroll down or correct the filter. And many people will have to do that many times a day.
2) Even with <=12 summaries it might be more convenient to just show the interesting ones because other shards just cluster the display and distract. E.g. many of my boards will have a summary if the queues for reading input are full. Unless I suspect problems I don't want to see this.
3) From a user interface perspective it's not intuitive to have a filter option and just filter for everything.
4) 6*2 summaries is to much for many screens, so you have to scroll within a page.

```
net = ...
```


I think I've narrowed things down a bit.

setup:

```python
import numpy as np
import dask.array as da
from distributed import Client
from sklearn.datasets import make_blobs

from dask_ml.metrics.pairwise import  rbf_kernel

N = 100
c = N // 8

X, y = make_blobs(n_samples=N, n_features=1)
X = da.from_array(X.reshape(-1, 1), chunks=c).persist()

keep = X[:10]
rest = X[10:]

A = rbf_kernel(keep)
B = rbf_kernel(keep, rest)

bb = da.random.uniform(size=B.shape, chunks=B.chunks)
cc = np.random.uniform(size=B.shape[1],)

r = (B * cc).sum(1)
s = (bb * cc).sum(1)
```

For larger problems, the computation of `r` leads to excessive memory usage, while the computation of `s` is fine.

Here's the graph for `r`

![bad](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1312546/37738273-77240b32-2d24-11e8-9fe7-b96ccf3730e2.png)

And here's the graph for `s`

![ok](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1312546/37738281-7de2150e-2d24-11e8-8ce7-03bfd73ea580.png)

Two things:

Computing just `B.sum(1).compute()`, i.e. `r` without multiplying by a NumPy array is fine. This makes me think that something can be improved within dask, though perhaps not.

For now, I'm hopeful we'll be able to rewrite `r` to fuse the kernel function and the multiplication into one operation within dask-ml.

I don't think I agree with you, as it seems like you're wanting to by-default redirect / break a C++ mechanism (mutable lvalue references) with `pybind` (defaulting to `copy`) rather than maintain it (`reference`) or augment it (`reference_internal`). (By break, I mean that you're not guaranteed to be modifying the instance you were passed for callback arguments, since they not may be registered in `pybind`, yadda yadda.).

I see what you mean by your example for a callback `[](py::function f) { f(HasElement().get()); }`, where a user may invoke a callback which is impure, and maintains a reference to a soon-to-be-invalidated temporary.

However, that seems like a *very* unusual edge case: the callback-invoking method uses a temporary value in C++, calls a method directly using `py::function` which is *directly* passed the return of a method returning a lvalue reference.

What I would see as more usual is that the user accepts a callback from Python, but then casts it to a `std::function<...>`, and then passes that to another, pure C++ callsite, where that callsite may have the temporary lvalue reference because, hey, it's pure C++. In this case, if the callback does not intend on modifying the value, then argument will be `T` or `const T&`, and then all the copies will be had on the pybind side, no harm no foul.
But, if the callback does modify the value, then it will be the same exact problem: The developer will have to explicitly dictate that they want a reference in the `py::cpp_function`, a downstream user will pass an impure callback that stores a global reference, and they'll be back at square one with the same segfault (with extra work on top of that).
(Additionally, if there is a segfault (at some point), it may be clearer that something has gone awry with referencing, rather than running the code to find out that your binding has not worked as expected. May that's just the masochist in me speaking, though...)

(As a minor aside, wouldn't `int&` be copied anyways, since it's a primitive / built-in pybind type? I know it's not super relevant to the discussion as it's a conceptual example, but I actually don't know.)

All that being said, ultimately a smaller workaround for me would be to wrap whatever methods, and replace any mutable rvalue references (arguments and return values) with pointers (possibly with a non-null check), as that'd be the behavior I'd want.

Thank you for the explanations and taking time to respond - sorry for the stubborness, just throwing in my 2 cents!

The implementation detail underlying this helps explain the behaviour a bit.  The main issue is that defaults get cast to Python objects before the method definition happens.  In this case, a nullptr is going to be cast to `None` when defining the method.  Later, when calling it from Python, we see the argument missing but that it has a default of `None`, so we copy `None` into the argument list before passing it on to the function.  That fails because there's no conversion from `None` to `set` (or the various other `stl` types).

Ideally we'd fail when you define the method, but that's not really feasible: the only way to know for sure whether `None` can be accepted is to pass it to the relevant type caster and see whether it accepts it or not.  (The `stl` ones don't).

To really solve this properly would need a sort of "do-you-accept-X?" method to type casters: then we could catch the error at definition time, rather than when calling the method.  (I'm not advocating for adding it: while it would help here, it would also make type casters more complex, which weighs against adding it).  Until/unless it supports such things, it's possible to pass defaults that can't actually be passed to the function.

(This also isn't restricted to `stl.h`: you could get the same error by specifying any default that can't be converted to the required type).

Today I also saw an upgrade for `python-cryptography` from `1.1.1-1~bpo8+1` to `1.3.4-1~bpo8+1` which creates some dependency conflicts that must be resolved manually. 

The first solution proposed wants to uninstall certbot which is obviously not what we want.

By going through the solutions proposed, I finally found one that seems correct, and which leads to promote two packages versions from stable to the jessie-backports:

```
python-pkg-resources:amd64 (5.5.1-1 --> 18.8-1~bpo8+1),
python-setuptools:amd64 (5.5.1-1 --> 18.8-1~bpo8+1)
```

because `python-cryptography 1.3.4-1~bpo8+1` depends on `python-setuptools >= 11.3` which is not available in stable.

After choosing this resolution I thought everything would go well, but still had the error 

```
pkg_resources.DistributionNotFound: The 'cffi>=1.4.1' distribution was not found and is required by cryptography
```

Finally I got it working by installing manually `python-cffi:amd64 (1.4.2-2~bpo8+1)`.

But this is weird because `python-cryptography 1.3.4-1~bpo8+1` depends on `python-cffi-backend-api-9729 (1.4.2-2~bpo8+1)` which I hoped would suffice.

In any case this issue seems to come from the packaging of `python-cryptography` and not certbot, since certbot only depends on `python-cryptography >= 0.7`

This should maybe go to `python-cryptography` upstream packaging team.

@aldanor, the underlying infrastructure is in place to specify resource requirements at task submission time. You're right that there is no real need to require all tasks that execute a given remote function to have the same resources requirements. So the current API is too inflexible.

I think the only thing preventing this feature is the lack of a clean API to specify resource requirements when submitting tasks, but I think you're right it would be useful. One workaround for now could be to just define multiple remote functions with different resource requirements. E.g.,

```python
def f():
    pass

f1 = ray.remote(resources={"Memory": 1})(f)
f2 = ray.remote(resources={"Memory": 2})(f)
f3 = ray.remote(resources={"Memory": 3})(f)
```

and then call whichever is appropriate.

Hi, @kszucs.

Yup, you are right. There is some inconsistencies in `Block.data` storing:
* When you emit `INSERT` query, data for insert is stored in block in row-wise way.
* On `SELECT` statement data received from CH is stored in `Block.data` in column-wise way.

That's why `get_rows` method is used to transpose received column-wise data to row-wise. This behavior should be split into different blocks later.

You can check this branch: https://github.com/mymarilyn/clickhouse-driver/tree/feature-deferred-rows-length-validation. There are some speed optimizations on `SELECT`.

If you want to do some research on performance you can use following profiling snippets:
```python
from clickhouse_driver import Client
c = Client('localhost')
%prun c.execute('SELECT * FROM large_table')
```

```python
from clickhouse_driver import Client
c = Client('localhost')
%prun c.execute('INSERT INTO test (a, b, c) VALUES', [(x, x, x) for x in range(N)])
```

When you start mixing in numpy and rpy2 it is not surprising that you can create conditions that crash. Jep is built off of python sub-interpreters which are a rarely used feature of cpython so extension writers often write code that is not compatible with sub-interpreters. There is not much we can do about this from the jep, we have open issues with numpy but it is fairly complex to fix them and there may be performance side affects so there is no activity to fix them. Our current workaround is the introduction of shared modules and in the next release of jep(3.8) I have added an option to use jep without sub-interpreters which should also help alleviate this problem but if you get the configuration wrong with native python extensions it can crash.

What was most interesting in the original post was that you managed to crash without using much python at all. I don't know how sbt works, but I think you were on the right track when you mentioned sbt keeping the python binaries around. I suspect that sbt is using the same process for both runs, however it attempted to unload the Jep class and reload it which causes it to reinitialize the python interpreter. So the first question is if you can verify this? Can you print out the process ID or something to verify that both runs are in the same process?

In theory Jep attempts to support this use case, when the first jep class is unloaded the JVM should call JNI_OnUnload, [which calls pyembed_shutdown](https://github.com/ninia/jep/blob/dev_3.7/src/jep/jep.c#L57), which should completely reset the python state. However I have never managed to get the JVM to actually unload in my testing. I suspect that the JVM cannot unload the Jep class because the [TopInterpreter Thread](https://github.com/ninia/jep/blob/dev_3.7/src/jep/Jep.java#L132) is still running. Looking at your hs_err_pid, I suspect that ` 0x00007f6edc01c800 JavaThread "Thread-1" daemon` is actually the first jep TopInterpreter still running and `0x00007f6ed80f3800 JavaThread "Thread-2" daemon` is the second TopInterpreter which crashes when it tries to reinitialize python. Although looking at the  initialization code, [this check](https://github.com/ninia/jep/blob/dev_3.7/src/jep/pyembed.c#L230) should actually prevent a crash during initialization, so I'm still not really sure exactly what is going on.

I suspect this problem may be resolved if you use forked JVMs in your SBT configuration, reading [some documentation](https://www.scala-sbt.org/1.0/docs/Running-Project-Code.html) it looks like the Jep background thread is exactly the type of use case that needs forking. It would still be good if we could fix this within Jep so please post any other information you find, but it would not be trivial if we really do need to handle jep class unloading so forking the JVM may be the most practical option.

We've thought about including the site-packages/jep folder in the jar about but don't currently support it.  If you submitted a pull request with a working change it'd get done faster.

An alternative is to use PYTHONPATH to tell Python where to look for the jep folder with the Python modules.  That way jep doesn't have to be installed in the same location as Python.

Problem is you run it you'll get [this](https://dl.dropboxusercontent.com/s/gg7yc9bbdn3a6mu/python_2018-05-25_18-28-26.png). Guess it's related to token priorities? What's the proper way to fix cases like these ones? The first idea it comes to my mind would be using regexes like `mat[234](x[234])?/.? ` but I'd prefer to keep a list of separated tokens so I can create the lark grammar automatically using some scripts. Please advice. B.
Merging and tagging a release.

This is expected and documented behavior. For most purposes, ASCII-only str are interchangeable with unicode, but have a much lower memory footprint and can be extracted from str document more quickly. If you expect only unicode output you simply have to provide unicode input.

It returns unicode all the time without speedups enabled because it's simply faster to do so. The input is always converted to unicode up front.
